mnemonics.title: OS FUNCTIONS
mnemonics.content: MEMORY PROCESS STORAGE PROTECTION SECURITY - Every Powerful System Needs Proper Security Measures. M for Memory Management, P for Process Management, S for Storage Management, P for Protection, S for Security.

mnemonics.title: OS GOALS
mnemonics.content: EFFECTIVE EFFICIENT USER-FRIENDLY - EEU. The OS makes the computer Effective (executes programs), Efficient (uses resources well), and User-friendly (easy to use).

mnemonics.title: SYSTEM CALL TYPES
mnemonics.content: PROCESS FILE MEMORY INFO DEVICE - PFMID. Process control, File management, Device management, Information maintenance, Communication.

mnemonics.title: OS COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content: KERNEL SHELL UTILITIES APPLICATIONS - KSUA. Kernel (core), Shell (interface), Utilities (tools), Applications (software).

acronyms.usage: The fundamental software managing hardware and providing services to applications
The main processor that executes instructions managed by the OS
Set of functions programs use to request services from the OS
Visual way to interact with the OS using icons and windows
Text-based interface to interact with the OS using commands
Firmware that initializes hardware before the OS loads
The gateway for programs to request OS services

visualTips.visualization: Picture an onion with layers: Hardware (innermost) -> Kernel -> System Calls -> Applications (outermost). Each layer provides services to the layer above it.
Think of the OS as a hotel concierge between a guest (user) and the hotel facilities (hardware). The concierge manages access so guests don't need to know how the hotel works.

Imagine a single waiter (CPU) serving multiple tables (programs). The waiter takes order from one table, then moves to another while the first table's food is being prepared (I/O), maximizing efficiency.
Draw a line dividing two rooms: User Mode (limited access, for regular apps) and Kernel Mode (full access, for OS core). System calls are the door between them.

analogies.analogy: Like a government - it manages resources (roads, hospitals), provides services (police, fire department), enforces rules (laws), and ensures fair access for all citizens (programs).

Like the brain of the computer - it controls everything, makes all major decisions, and runs in the most privileged state. Just as the brain cannot be directly controlled consciously, the kernel runs autonomously.
Like a project manager in an office - creates tasks (processes), assigns resources, switches between tasks to keep everyone working, and resolves conflicts.
Like a library librarian - keeps track of which books (memory pages) are checked out, where they are located, and ensures fair access for all readers.
Like a filing cabinet with folders and files - organizes data in a hierarchical structure, keeps track of what goes where, and prevents unauthorized access.
